标题：🔌 万亿美元的关闭开关

正文：欢迎阅读《他们如何赚钱》精品版。超过30万订阅者依赖我们的商业和投资洞察。若您错过了以下内容：⚡ FIFA如何赚钱 🚀 SpaceX如何赚钱 🔍 谷歌为何出售价值850亿美元的股份 📈 股票回购与股息解析 立即订阅

创纪录的IPO，随后又被关停
SpaceX刚完成史上最大规模的IPO，筹资约750亿美元，估值接近1.8万亿美元。股票在首两天已上涨超40%，市值突破2.5万亿美元，几乎与亚马逊持平。但这仅是开场戏。Anthropic（安斯博）和OpenAI（开放AI）已递交保密上市文件，为未来数月的大规模IPO奠定基础。两家公司都有望市值突破1万亿美元。Anthropic的私募估值已跃升至9650亿美元，超过OpenAI，成为纸面上全球最有价值的独立AI实验室。但该公司刚收到美国出口管制指令，限制其两个最强模型的使用。由于无法实时筛选用户，Anthropic被迫在全球范围内禁用这两款模型。每家大型AI公司都在决定是自行研发前沿模型（如Anthropic、OpenAI和Google）还是租用已有模型。直到上周，这主要是成本与控制权的权衡。如今多了一个变量：监管关闭的风险。

今日要点：
- 前沿AI公司迎来极差的一周
- 构建模型的风险加大
- 苹果的意外对冲

items.author: https://www.chinatalk.media/feed
items.title: Emergency Pod: Claude Fable
items.textEn: Last Tuesday, Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5 went live as the most powerful model in the world. By Friday it was dark, pulled offline after a private "is-informed" letter from Commerce’s Bureau of Industry and Security restricted foreign access to it, apparently triggered by a jailbreak concern and a phone call from Amazon’s Andy Jassy. An administration that spent two years making fun of the Biden admin for regulatory AI overreach showed they really just caught the AI safety bug. To make sense of it, we’re joined by Chris McGuire , longtime State Department and White House civil servant who worked AI and chips now at CFR. We discuss… The 5:21 PM letter that took the world’s most powerful model offline in a single day Why the ‘let it rip’ admin pivoted to mandatory AI regulation overnight. The overseas-subsidiary loophole, the Sunday emergency patch, and the foundry gap still wide open Listen now on your favorite podcast app . Live Tuesday, Dark by Friday Jordan Schneider: My mayor Muslim My bagel Jewish. Fable is fried. Knicks in five. Congratulations to the New York Knickerbockers and the city of New York on their first championship in 53 years. And a hearty — perhaps — message of concern for the Trump administration’s Bureau of Industry and Security at Commerce, which has been taking care of America’s lead about as well as De’Aaron Fox took care of the basketball over the past two weeks. To discuss why Trump took Fable offline, what’s going on with chip and AI loopholes, and what this all means for the future of AI development in America and beyond, we’re joined today by native New Yorker and longtime State Department and White House civil servant covering AI, chips, and whatnot. You know him, you love him — Chris McGuire. Welcome back to the show. Chris McGuire: I’ve been a Knicks fan much longer than I’ve been any of those things, so that’s the most important thing right now. But yeah — at your urging, had to wear the jersey to celebrate. Won’t be taking it off for at least a week, or until it smells. Jordan Schneider: Just a brief recap. We recorded a ModelTalk episode on Thursday of last week where we were all talking about our Fable impressions — Anthropic’s latest model. Now we have a nice little note when you go to Claude.ai saying Claude Fable 5 is currently unavailable. Why is that? Still sort of unclear, but the facts we know: at some point on Thursday, Andy Jassy gets on the phone with someone in the Trump administration and says, hey, there’s something fishy about this whole Claude model. We found some jailbreaks which can do something. There was some sort of communication between Anthropic and various corners of the Trump administration. And then at 5:21, we get a new export control — and I hand over the reins of this narration to Chris. So what did Commerce put out, and what were the implications? Chris McGuire: Yeah, so there was an is-informed letter — some of the details are still not publicly reported, but basically it was a private letter sent to Anthropic that one way or another regulated the export of the model, the remote access of the model, or the ability to access the model via an API from other countries, or from foreign nationals inside the United States. The Commerce Department has regulated the export of models before, in the Biden administration. The Trump administration actually, interestingly, is not enforcing those particular export controls, but has now introduced these. The authority to regulate foreign persons is called a deemed export control. In the Biden administration we actually generally exempted most of the emerging tech controls from deemed export controls, because of concerns over how it would impact business in the United States. But you can say that a foreign national inside the United States requires a license to receive a good that is controlled for export outside the United States. And then it’s a little unclear how all this fits together in a way that actually regulates API access. There are probably ways you could do it, but without seeing the letter, it’s a little hard to tell. The net result is that it was illegal to export the model, or use the model, by any foreigner — either outside or inside the United States — which obviously made it very hard to serve the model even in the United States, because you’d have to verify who end users were. Presumably they’re actually probably employees at Anthropic who are foreign nationals, who all of a sudden are restricted from accessing the model that they work on. So this obviously put the use of it to a stop. And now it seems like there are discussions going on today to try to resolve this. Jordan Schneider: So first off — is Andy Jassy the Jeremy Sochan of this story? Jeremy Sochan, of course, drafted by the Spurs, worked there for three years, got cut, shows up to the Knicks and wins a championship by spilling all of their internal workings and secrets. Chris McGuire: I don’t know what the motivation is. It’ll be interesting to see, on the Knicks side, how much of a behind-the-scenes role Sochan played in the championship, and how much we had all the intel due to a crafty signing mid-year. Underreported part of the series. Jordan Schneider: Or did the Knicks get access to Mythos during the Glasswing window, use it to hack Wemby’s email, get all the dirt on him in order to whisper disturbing things while he was taking free throws? All right, we’ll stop with this. Sorry. Chris McGuire: Wouldn’t be the first time a sports team hacked another one for competitive advantage. But yeah, I don’t think it happened here. Jordan Schneider: So clearly there’s an overlay of bad blood, or at least no love lost, between this administration and Anthropic. But the more remarkable piece of this story from my perspective: you had this administration talking for two years about how they wanted capitalism to run free, that AI shouldn’t be regulated, that this was important to American development and leadership, and that those safety nicks in the Biden administration were really overreacting. And then you go from that to an executive order which was going to be not all that tight — watered down even more when it came out two weeks ago — to all of a sudden, over the course of apparently 24 hours, deciding that the most powerful model in the world, which is an instantiation of America’s lead over the rest of the world, needs to no longer have access. So, Chris, I’m curious for your reflections on that arc, and maybe what it tells you about the future of AI policymaking. The Sea Change Chris McGuire: The particulars of this individual incident aside, the administration very clearly has fundamentally changed its policy on AI over the last few months. I think the Mythos release really did cause a sea change in Washington on AI policy. The policy has not been "let the private sector rip" for four months or so. I think you saw the culmination of that in the AI executive order, which put forward a voluntary safety regime — but ultimately it’s obviously kind of mandatory. I think this basically shows that it’s mandatory. So it’s logical. This is a real problem, and the administration is realizing that AI regulation, and the very powerful capabilities these models have, requires regulation. To the administration’s credit, they’ve recognized that and pivoted, at least in a broad philosophical sense. They haven’t pivoted all elements of their policy, and they’re also moving in ways that are very reactive and fairly chaotic. What’s clearly needed is a policy that’s not just reactive — where there’s a framework set out of, here’s the expectations, here’s how we regulate things domestically, here’s how we regulate them internationally, here’s what people can and can’t do. And it has a degree of durability. I say a degree, because this is regulating at the frontier and it’s hard. We did this in the Biden administration, and there were degrees to which we’d have to update the controls, and then business would yell at us and say, well, this isn’t predictable. And we’d say, we’re doing our best — we’re trying to create as predictable an environment as we can while responding to national security risks. But we did have some degree of a framework out there. The Trump administration needs to do that. They need to be clear about what is and isn’t allowable domestically. There are apparently some standards in the AI EO for how they’d do model evaluation, but they’re classified — probably helpful if those were public. But regardless, you could work with it. There aren’t that many companies. You also need a corresponding international strategy, of which I think we have none. How are you going to ensure AI infrastructure globally is safe? How are you going to make sure that other countries, when they start making this level of capability — which they will, very soon — are not used against us in the ways the administration is correctly worried that people could inadvertently use current-generation models against us too? There needs to be a comprehensive strategy. We’re just not seeing that. And I feel like that’s a trope in Washington — "we just need a strategy" is what everyone says to anything, so I almost hesitate to say it. But export controls in particular are a powerful, useful tool. A tool that can ensure technology is given to the people you want it given to, and create a kind of international nonproliferation regime to a degree. But instead we have this insane situation where right now Canadians are prevented from accessing these US models, but meanwhile the chips that make the models are free to be sent to China because of loopholes the administration created. So look, I hope the administration recognizes that AI regulation holistically is important, and that this is an indicator export controls are an important and powerful tool…
items.summaryZh: 文章讨论了Anthropic最新的Claude Fable 5模型因美国商务部出口管制信函在上线数日后被迫下线，突显了美国对最前沿AI技术监管政策的急速转向，对投资者理解监管风险及行业政策环境至关重要。
items.translationZh:
标题：紧急舱：Claude Fable

正文：上周二，Anthropic（安特罗匹克）的Claude Fable 5作为全球最强模型上线。到了周五，它因商务部工业与安全局（Bureau of Industry and Security）发出的私人“已通知”（is‑informed）信函被迫下线，显然是因担心模型被越狱（jailbreak）以及亚马逊CEO Andy Jassy 的一次电话所致。此前两年一直嘲笑拜登政府对AI监管过度的行政部门，这一次真的抓住了AI安全的虫子。为了解析此事，我们请来了Chris McGuire——长期在国务院和白宫任职、曾负责AI和芯片政策、现任美国外交关系委员会（CFR）研究员。我们讨论了……

‑ 5:21 PM的信函在一天之内让全球最强模型下线的原因
‑ 为何本来“让私营部门自由发挥”的政府一夜之间转向强制性AI监管
‑ 海外子公司漏洞、周日紧急补丁以及仍然宽阔的代工（foundry）缺口

现在就用你喜欢的播客应用收听吧。上周二上线，周五下线

Jordan Schneider：我的市长是穆斯林，我的贝果是犹太人。Fable被炸了。Knicks在5场赛制中赢了。恭喜纽约尼克斯和纽约市，53年后首次夺冠。并向特朗普政府商务部的工业与安全局（Bureau of Industry and Security）发出——或许是——关切信息，该局在过去两周对美国的领袖维护得像De’Aaron Fox在篮球场上那样糟糕。为讨论特朗普为何让Fable下线、芯片与AI的漏洞以及这对美国乃至全球AI发展意味着什么，我们今天邀请了本土纽约人、长期在国务院和白宫负责AI、芯片等事务的公务员Chris McGuire。你认识他，你也喜欢他——Chris McGuire。欢迎回到节目。

Chris McGuire：我作为尼克斯球迷的时间比我担任那些职务的时间还长，所以这才是现在最重要的事。但好吧——应你的要求，我必须穿上球衣庆祝。至少一周内，甚至更久都不会脱下来，除非它闻起来……

Jordan Schneider：简要回顾一下。我们上周四录制了一期ModelTalk，讨论了对Fable的初步印象——Anthropic的最新模型。现在我们访问Claude.ai时会看到Claude Fable 5当前不可用。原因是什么？仍不完全清楚，但我们掌握的事实如下：在周四的某个时点，Andy Jassy给特朗普政府的某位官员打电话，说Claude模型有点可疑。我们发现了一些可以进行越狱的方式。Anthropic与特朗普政府的多个部门之间有过沟通。随后在5:21，我们收到一份新的出口管制信函——我把叙事的接力棒交给Chris。

Chris McGuire：是的，这是一封已通知（is‑informed）信函——细节仍未公开，但基本上是一封私密信件，要求Anthropic对模型的出口、远程访问或通过API向其他国家或美国境内的外国人士提供服务进行管制。商务部在拜登政府时期已经对模型实施过出口管制。特朗普政府实际上并未执行这些特定的出口管制，但现在引入了新的规定。对外国个人的管制权被称为视同出口管制（deemed export control）。在拜登政府时期，我们基本上对大多数前沿技术的视同出口管制予以豁免，因为担心会影响美国本土业务。但可以说，外国人在美国境内获取受出口管制的产品仍需许可证。

具体如何适用于API访问还有些模糊。没有看到信函内容，很难判断。但结果是，未经授权的外国人——无论在美国境外还是境内——都被禁止使用该模型，这显然使得即使在美国本土也难以提供服务，因为必须验证最终用户的身份。很可能这些被限制的是Anthropic的外籍员工，他们突然被禁止访问自己正在研发的模型，导致模型使用被迫停止。目前看来，相关各方正努力寻找解决方案。

Jordan Schneider：那Andy Jassy是不是这件事的Jeremy Sochan？Jeremy Sochan被马刺队选中，效力三年后被裁，随后加入尼克斯并通过泄露内部信息夺冠。

Chris McGuire：我不知道动机是什么。很有趣的是，看尼克斯方面到底有多少幕后操作，以及我们是否因为一次巧妙的中期签约而获得了情报。

Jordan Schneider：或者尼克斯在Glasswing窗口期间获取了Mythos，黑进了Wembanyama的邮箱，获取了他的所有信息，以此在罚球时进行干扰？好吧，这里先不继续。抱歉。

Chris McGuire：这并不是第一次体育团队为了竞争优势而进行黑客攻击。但我认为这里并未发生。

Jordan Schneider：显然，这个政府与Anthropic之间关系紧张，甚至可以说是互不相爱。但在我看来更值得注意的是：这届政府过去两年大喊资本主义自由、反对AI监管，声称这对美国的技术领先至关重要，并指责拜登政府的安全担忧是小题大做。结果在一次行政命令（AI Executive Order）本来就已经被削弱的情况下，竟在24小时内决定让全球最强模型——美国在全球AI竞争中的领军象征——不能再被访问。这说明了什么？

Chris McGuire：就此个案而言，政府在过去几个月里显然彻底改变了AI政策。我认为Mythos的发布在华盛顿引发了海潮式（sea change）的政策转变。此前四个月左右的“让私营部门自由发挥”政策已不复存在。我们在AI执行令（Executive Order）中看到的所谓自愿安全框架，实际上已经变成了强制性的。也就是说，监管是必须的。政府已经认识到这一点，并在哲学层面上做出了转向。虽说他们并未在所有细节上转变，而且很多举措显得被动且混乱，但至少表明了监管的必要性。更重要的是，需要一套不仅仅是被动反应的政策框架，明确国内外的监管标准、允许与禁止的行为，并具备一定的持久性。因为在前沿技术领域制定规则本身就很困难。拜登政府也曾尝试建立这样的框架，只是企业会抱怨缺乏可预测性。我们需要在保持安全的同时，提供相对可预期的环境。特朗普政府也必须如此。虽然AI执行令中对模型评估有标准，但目前是机密的，公开会更有帮助。行业公司并不多，但仍需明确国内哪些行为是允许的、哪些是被禁止的。同时，需要对应的国际战略，而我们目前几乎没有。如何确保全球AI基础设施安全？当其他国家很快也能打造同等能力时，如何防止这些技术被用于对我们不利的目的？这需要全面的策略。华盛顿常挂在口头的“我们只需要一个战略”，但实际上落实很少。出口管制是强有力的工具，能够确保技术只交付给我们希望交付的对象，并在一定程度上形成国际防扩散（non‑proliferation）体系。但现在出现了荒唐的局面：加拿大人被阻止使用美国的模型，而制造这些模型的芯片却因行政漏洞可以自由出口到中国。希望政府认识到，AI监管必须整体考虑，出口管制是重要且有力的手段……

items.title: The Geography of Time
items.textEn: Programming Note : As a reminder, every Sunday, I write pieces that are predominantly based on personal experiences which may or may not be loosely connected with investing. If you are reading MBI Deep Dives everyday, I think it would be rather useful for my readers to understand my personal lens a bit better since that presumably affects (at least in some capacity) the way I analyze businesses as well. After three and half years, I went to my home country: Bangladesh with my wife and son Nile. We were concerned how one and half year old Nile would cope with the grueling 20-hour flight from SFO to Dhaka (with six hour transit in Dubai), but thankfully it was uneventful. He was curious and tad bit excited at the first sight of Bangladesh. While I spent my first two and half decades in Bangladesh, it’s been almost a decade since I visited Bangladesh during the summer. Most people living abroad tend to visit the country during winter as the summer heat can be quite punishing. We still decided to go for it because we wanted to celebrate Eid with our family. However, right after landing, the humid, musty Bangladesh summer got us all drenched in sweat while rummaging for our luggage. I wasn’t bothered by the heat; if anything, I was almost nostalgic. The scent of hot, humid Bangladesh summer can be unpleasant to the unfamiliar but to someone who grew up there, it was a return to the familiar milieu that got lost for a decade. I was a little worried how Nile would handle the heat, but even he appeared to be nonchalant about it and was busy watching people. For the uninitiated, Bangladesh is the size of the New York state but has half the population of the US. So, if you are into peoplewatching, Bangladesh has plenty to offer! As we headed from the airport to my brother’s place, I was pleasantly surprised by the elevated expressways which likely cut down the time to our destination by at least half (if not more). I often joke with my friends that perhaps half the people who leave Bangladesh decide to do so while being stuck in traffic as they wonder there must be more to life than spending two-three hours everyday in traffic. I only learned to drive after moving to the US as we never had a car growing up in Bangladesh. But now that I know how to drive, it was quite a nerve wracking experience just sitting in the car in Bangladesh. There is traffic everywhere and hardly anyone is following any traffic rule. Frankly speaking, I felt if you are commuting in Bangladesh, you should consider everyday a little miracle that you made it back in one piece. After spending just one night in Dhaka, we traveled the next day to Bogura , the small city in Northern Bangladesh where both my wife and I grew up. Nile received quite the reception from both sets of grandparents. Thankfully, since both grandparents’ place is literally two-minute walking distance from each other, Nile was busy hopping from one grandparent to another everyday. My romanticism about Bangladesh started to fade the more time I spent in Bogura. The reality is even though I migrated to the Northern Hemisphere back in 2017, it was a long road of struggle to make it as a first generation of immigrant. But as things started to fall into places in the last couple of years, I discovered that I have become surprisingly used to certain amenities in life. It was almost impossible to maintain my daily 10k steps habit as the summer heat and dusty air reminded me that I was giving myself too much credit for being able to build a good habit. Perhaps California weather is simply nice enough to make it easier to stick to such habit. Moreover, as I was gaining weight after consuming copious amount of unhealthy Bangladeshi food every meal, I started googling about GLP-1. The worst was, of course, that my sleep schedule got completely destroyed and by the time I could get back to some normalcy, I had to hop onto my return flight to California. It also didn’t help that I probably wasn’t in the greatest frame of mind while in Bangladesh. I was in the middle of two big work related stressors after discovering the myriad tax related complexities for owning a company incorporated in Canada but living in the US. I decided to move my business from Canada to the US which forced me to migrate all the subscriptions from Stripe Canada to Stripe US. This proved to be significantly more challenging than I expected going in and I was coordinating among Ghost, Substack, Stripe, and a developer I hired for the project. I know the payment stocks are getting killed in the market, but the whole experience reminded me that the last thing I want to change again is my payments infrastructure. Anyways, I was particularly curious about how people in Bangladesh are using AI. The fact that AI is a big deal is not news to anyone I interacted in Bangladesh. Everyone is familiar with ChatGPT, but Gemini was also frequently mentioned. While almost everyone uses the free versions, there are a handful of people who mentioned they pay for the subscription. However, they don’t buy the subscription directly from ChatGPT. Apparently, there are some “Facebook Groups” which sell ChatGPT subscriptions. They will give you a specific email address and password with access to ChatGPT subscription for ~$3-5/month. Presumably, if they give the same email address to ten people, you can see these resellers are making handsome margin (ironically almost certainly far higher than OpenAI itself). Of course, the main downside for such “subscribers” is they have no privacy as their queries can be seen by ten other people who have the access to the same account. If large part of the world needs to make a trade-off between privacy and intelligence, it’s not particularly a difficult choice for many people. However, almost everyone I talked to seems to be still confined to the “chatbot” era of AI. I haven’t heard anyone mentioning “agent” once while discussing AI in Bangladesh. Speaking of Facebook, I came across another story from a friend that is less than ideal for…Meta. My friend ordered some fish via a Facebook page. The payment went through but the products were never delivered. When he dug into see why he fell for a scam, it turns out someone cloned the Facebook page he was looking for and the scammer even managed to get thousands of likes (almost similar to the original page) presumably by promoting ads (or who knows maybe via “click farms”). What surprised my friend was that he actually spoke with the seller over the phone and confirmed the order only after speaking directly to the seller. You probably guessed where it’s going…the scammer used AI to clone the voice of the original seller! You can only imagine how much money will be lost to such sophisticated scams in the age of AI. Unfortunately, AI must be a godsend to the scammers all over the world and it’s going to be a long, difficult fight among the platforms, scammers, and users. Even in the age of AI, there are some things that never seem to change. I visited my paternal grandparents village in Bogura. I still marvel thinking about the fact that my paternal grandparent was born in 1920 and died in 2000 in the same village and yet the very land beneath his feet belonged to three different nations in his lifetime. He was born at the time the British still ruled the subcontinent, then the village was part of Pakistan after the British left in 1947, and then it changed again to be part of Bangladesh when the country became independent in 1971. Whenever I want to imagine how dynamic the world around you can be, I just remind this personal anecdote to me. Anyways, this village got access to electricity only a decade ago and every time I go there, I am struck by how unevenly distributed time really is. If California represents the vanguard of 2026, walking through this village feels like stepping through a portal into a different century entirely. I captured the below with my Meta Glasses while strolling through the village: Our summer trip to Bangladesh was sweaty, and logistically chaotic, but seeing Nile bounce between his grandparents made the expensive 20-hour flight worth it. The romanticism of my youth might have faded into the practical realities of adulthood, but the roots remain. As we settled back into our routine under the beautiful California sun, I realized time moves differently depending on where you stand and I’m grateful for the chance to have stood in both places this summer. items.summaryZh: 作者回顾了他与家人最近从美国前往孟加拉国的旅行，描绘了当地夏季的酷热、拥堵的交通和不发达的基础设施，并讨论了孟加拉网民对ChatGPT等AI工具的使用方式以及潜在的隐私风险。作者还具体提到在当地看到的AI诈骗案例，强调AI技术既带来便利也带来安全隐患。此文从个人视角呈现技术与日常运作差异，提示投资者在全球化商业与技术布局中需关注地方差距与监管环境。
items.translationZh: 编程笔记：每周日，我都会撰写主要基于个人经历的文章，可能与投资略有松散关联。我思考如果你每天阅读《MBI 深度探究》会更好，让读者了解我的个人视角，因为这会在某种程度上影响我对企业的分析。三年半后，我和妻子、儿子Nile一起去了我的祖国孟加拉国。我们担心一岁半的Nile是否能应对从旧金山（SFO）飞往达卡（Dhaka）的20小时长途航班（在迪拜停留六小时），但幸运的是旅程顺利。Nile对孟加拉的第一眼印象很好奇，也有点激动。过去我在孟加拉度过了两个半十年的时光，已经十年多没有去那里夏天出行——多数在外生活的人会在冬天回国，因为夏季热度令人难受。我们依旧决定去，因为想与家人一起庆祝开斋节。刚落地，潮湿、闷热的孟加拉夏天让我们全身湿透，忙乱找行李。我对热并不在意，反而几乎怀念那种味道。对在外人来说，潮湿闷热的气味可能不舒服，但对自己成长之地而言，这是十年前失去的熟悉氛围。虽然担心Nile是否能应付炎热，却发现他似乎不在乎，专注于观察人们。对初学者来说，孟加拉的面积相当于纽约州，但人口只有美国的一半。如果你喜欢观察人群，孟加拉有足够多给你带来惊奇。我们从机场前往兄弟家，意外发现高铁高架高速公路，估计至少减少了一半旅行时间（甚至更多）。我常跟朋友打趣，或许因为离开孟加拉的人数之大，他们抵车辆拥堵又不得不出行，想象生活中还有更丰富的东西。直到搬到美国后，我才学会开车，因为在孟加拉长大时从未拥有一辆车。但是现在懂了怎么开车，我在孟加拉的车里度过了一段紧张的经历。交通无处不在，几乎没人遵守交通规则。说实话，我觉得如果你在孟加拉通勤，应该把每天安全到达当作小奇迹。一次在达卡过夜后，我们第二天前往北部的博格拉（Bogura），那里既是我和妻子的家乡。Nile受到两边祖父母热烈的迎接。值得庆幸的是，双亲祖父母的住所相距不到两分钟步行距离，Nile每天蹦蹦跳跳，像是小婴儿！我对孟加拉的浪漫情怀随着时间慢慢消失。现实是，虽然我在2017年迁往北半球，但成为第一代移民的道路一段很长。如今在此后的几年里，一切终于迎来了安定，我驚人地发现自己对生活中的某些便利已适应。夏天高温和尘土飞扬让我几乎无法维持每日至少10,000步的目标，意识到自己给自己太大的信心。我在加州的天气让我更容易坚持习惯。与此同时，我因吃了大量不健康的孟加拉餐而体重增加，开始谷歌搜索GLP-1（GLP-1，胰高血糖素样肽‑1，糖尿病药物）。最糟糕的是，我的睡眠时间完全被打乱，直到到达加州前，我还在努力调整。更糟糕的是，我可能在孟加拉时精神状态并不最佳。我正处在两大工作压力之中——因在加拿大注册公司在美国居住，面对税务复杂性，我决定将业务从加拿大转到美国，并将 Stripe 加拿大账户迁移到 Stripe 美国账户。这比我想象中更具挑战性，因为我需要协调 Ghost（Ghost，写作平台）、Substack（Substack，写作与订阅平台）、Stripe（Stripe，美国支付公司）以及我雇佣的开发人员。支付行业正在下跌，整个经验提醒我我想要的最后一点是保持支付基础设施不变。接下来，我特别好奇孟加拉人如何使用 AI。AI 仍是大家讨论的焦点。每个人都熟知 ChatGPT（ChatGPT，OpenAI 的聊天机器人），但 Gemini（Gemini，Google 的人工智能产品）也被频繁提及。几乎所有人都在使用免费版本，但有少数人提到会付费订阅。然而，他们并不直接从 ChatGPT 订阅，而是通过一些“Facebook 群组”销售 ChatGPT 订阅。他们会给你一个特定的电子邮件地址和密码，月费约 3‑5 美元。显而易见，如果同一邮箱向十个人共享，你可以看到这些转卖者赚取可观利润（与 OpenAI 本身的利润相差悬殊）。当然，对这些订阅者而言，主要缺点是隐私——因为同一账号的十个用户可以看到彼此的查询。若大部分人面临隐私与智能的权衡，许多人并不难做出选择。几乎所有人都还停留在“聊天机器人”时代，我未听到任何人谈论“代理”一词。谈到 Facebook，我听遇到一则奇怪的诈骗故事。我的朋友通过 Facebook 页面订购鱼。付款成功，却从未收到货物。当他调查为何会被骗时，原来有人复制了他想要的 Facebook 页面并成功获取数千赞（与原页面近似），可能通过广告普及或“点击农场”。出乎他意料的是，他与卖家通话确认订单后，竟然对方用 AI 克隆了原卖家的声音！你可以想象，在 AI 时代，类似的诈骗将使数百万人蒙受巨额损失。可惜 AI 同样是诈骗分子的大帮手，日后在平台、诈骗者和用户之间将展开艰难斗争。即便在 AI 时代，某些事情依旧不变。走进我父亲祖母的村庄，我仍在惊叹自 1920 年出生、2000 年逝世、同一块土地在三国之中转手的历史。当时英国殖民统治结束后，殖民地成为巴基斯坦的一部分，1971 年独立后又变为孟加拉国。我想象不平衡的“时间”——当加州代表 2026 年的技术前沿时，走进这片土地像穿过不同的世纪。用我的 Meta Glasses（Meta Glasses，Meta 的智能眼镜）捕捉了以下画面：我们的夏季之旅在汗水中、起伏中却让 20 小时的高价机票值得。青春的浪漫或许已随现实成人主义而淡化，但根系仍在。回到加州的美好阳光下，我意识到时间随地点而异，感激能够在本夏日站在两地。订阅者可获得每日日志及五年以上的深度探究，即 65+ 公司的完整分析与财务模型。每日日记只是在完成深度探究时的思路发声。订阅当前投资组合时，请注意这些并非我的买卖建议，只是披露，以便你评估我可能存在的偏见。本人的钱包公开如下：

items.title: Notes on Egypt
items.textEn: Source . When my driver picked me up to take me to the New Capital, he was certain there had been a booking mistake. "Are you sure you want to go this way? I can take you to the pyramids or Alexandria instead." There was no mistake. But driving there, past a mural of Egypt’s President Sisi every few hundred meters (sunglasses on, pointing at renderings of his new mega-city) I didn’t know what to expect. There’s only so much you can understand about the New Capital from reading about it online. Almost all the pictures are airbrushed or shot to show only the skyline without the heaps of rubble just to the side. Even the name is confusing. After years of competitions, jury deliberations, and proposed names like "Kemet," "Wedian," and "Memphis," the city is still technically just the ‘New Administrative Capital.’ ​ Cairo isn’t sinking like Jakarta, and nobody seems to know for certain why the New Capital exists. The official reason is to relieve congestion in a city whose metropolitan population is ~20 million. Yes, parts of Cairo are incredibly dense and congested, but there are also parts that are quite nice and spacious. The New Capital isn’t just a convenient annex to Cairo’s outskirts. It’s in the middle of the desert and took us an hour to get there and, according to my driver, "two hours, if there’s traffic." On the night of Eid al-Fitr, Cairo was filled with swarms of teenage boys eager to practice their English with lost-looking foreigners. So I asked them why. Subscribe now Based on my highly formal street survey, the most common response from teenagers was that "Sisi wants to show he’s got big balls." Someone told me he wanted to be like Khufu, who built the first and biggest of the Giza pyramids. A lady who worked at one of the many lingerie shops in Cairo told me, with a look of historical resignation, "Egyptians have always liked to spend our money building great big things in the middle of nowhere." A surprisingly common response was that Sisi built the capital far away and with wide boulevards to make it revolution-proof. In a dense city like Cairo, crowds can surround a government building, but in a capital of eight-lane mega-highways, you’d need a car just to stage a protest. Several people also noted that it’s a place for the rich to escape to. This is expressed most clearly in Madinaty, the gated suburb near the New Capital that, driving through it, makes you question whether you are still in Egypt or in the suburbs of Southern California. Madinaty, a suburb 45 minutes from Cairo for for Egypt’s elite. The photo doesn’t show that it’s surrounded by desert. Source . "It’s okay," said a man in a dusted suit. "China is building it for us." Entering the New Capital [Dune theme song plays] The entrance to the New Capital is a gigantic gate rising up from miles of sand in all directions. Everywhere you go in the New Capital can basically be summarized the same way: the biggest, most gaudy thing you’ve ever heard of, surrounded by sand and rubble. Entrance gate. Source . "That is the largest mosque in Africa," my driver told me, gesturing with his cigarette at a pair of Redwood-like Minarets rising out of the sand. When I asked to stop for a photo, he didn’t take the next off-ramp or even pull over to the shoulder. He simply came to a full stop in the middle of the highway while I got out and took my picture. The mosque was empty. Largest mosque in Africa. Source . We also passed: Sports City, built to anchor Egypt’s Olympic and World Cup bids, housing more than 22 facilities, including a stadium with nearly 94,000 seats, the largest in Egypt and the second largest on the continent. Sports City. Source . Knowledge City , a $950 million tech campus, the government bills as a node in a future digital economy (reminiscent of Zhongguancun in Beijing). Knowledge City. Source . Sisi’s new presidential palace , an enormous disc-shaped structure, said to be shaped in the style of the ancient Egyptian sun god. Presidential palace. Source . An endless row of government ministry offices , each in its own monstrously large and gold-laced building, some with titles so specialized it was hard to imagine the staff filling a single floor, let alone these giant structures. Green River , the capital’s park with fields of glowing green grass running through the desertous landscape, SIX times the size of Central Park. Green River concept rendering. Source . What thematically unifies these monuments is the windswept sand that coats them all, aging the white facades as if the paint has not fully dried. Rubble and half-finished construction stretch in every direction, and stray dogs sleep in the middle of the highways. My movie comp: Bladerunner 2049 . This is no more true than in the downtown, with its cluster of towers, including the tallest building in Africa, built by the Chinese. Tallest building in Africa. China The city was originally supposed to be built by Capital City Partners, a private real estate firm led by an Emirati businessman, but that arrangement collapsed in 2015. Egypt then signed a new agreement with China State Construction Engineering Corporation (CSCEC) to build and finance the administrative core, though CSCEC ultimately narrowed its involvement to the central business district. Europeans are involved too. Siemens built much of the power infrastructure, while the monorail is being built by Alstom (UK), though China is the biggest player. Share The New Capital is being marketed as a "smart city," which in practice means a city pre-wired for surveillance infrastructure, data integration, and perhaps autonomous vehicles. I’ve written before about how China’s approach to autonomous vehicles , at home and abroad, is about more than just exporting cars but about exporting an entire infrastructure stack, reshaping the built environment so that the technology fits cleanly into it rather than the other way around. I didn’t spot any Chinese AVs testing in the New Capital (I didn’t see many vehicles generally), but the roads themselves still showed the foundations. Compared to Cairo’s honking chaos, they were wide, clearly marked, and fitted with cameras and sensors at the finished intersections. A peeling welcome sign in downtown. This is increasingly the playbook. Indonesia is building Nusantara on Borneo to replace Jakarta, with the same decongest-the-old-city rationale, heavy Chinese involvement in its smart city and surveillance infrastructure, and the same ghost-town-in-progress atmosphere, now compounded by a new president who has quietly cut the budget in half and moved on. Senegal built Diamniadio , thirty kilometers outside Dakar, with heavy Chinese financing. Malaysia’s Forest City , developed by Chinese firm Country Garden on reclaimed land near Singapore, is now empty after Malaysian political backlash and capital controls killed demand. (It is reported that Forest City was not targeted at local Malaysians but at upper-class Chinese buyers looking to park wealth abroad, for whom the seafront properties were relatively affordable compared to coastal cities like Shanghai.) China, of course, is doing this on its home turf. Xiong’an , announced in 2017 as Xi Jinping’s personal urban legacy project sits 100 kilometers southwest of Beijing, planned to absorb the capital’s non-essential functions and eventually house 5 million people. Seven years on, it doesn’t seem to be going great. To be fair, there are versions of these neo-cities that work out. Astana replaced Almaty as Kazakhstan’s capital in 1997 and is more or less functional, which is presumably why the Egyptian government sought advice from its planners. But the big question is who bears the cost if it doesn’t go well. For Egypt and countries like it, the answer is obvious. For China, it’s more complicated than it might seem. Subscribe now Chinese SOE construction firms get paid to build, not to populate. Their revenue is front-loaded, and whether the city ever fills up is largely the host country’s problem. The Belt and Road Initiative, the Digital Silk Road, all the framework agreements and memoranda of understanding make the most sense not as instruments of grand strategic maneuvering but as the answer to a simpler question: What do you do with the most formidable construction apparatus in human history once you’ve run out of things to build at home ? The risk to China comes mainly when host countries can’t service their debt (which does happen, as Sri Lanka demonstrated , when it handed over a strategically located port to a Chinese state firm on a 99-year lease after defaulting on its loans) but even then, China has often been able to extract concessions, renegotiate on favorable terms, or simply absorb the loss as the cost of keeping its construction sector employed and its political relationships warm. In Egypt’s case, the dynamic is already visible. In 2023, China converted over $9.4 billion in Egyptian debt into developmental projects and investments, acquiring some state assets in the process. If this is truly the Chinese century, build-outs like this are its texture. The grand geoeconomic questions are worth asking, but driving past millions of empty residential units, I found myself returning to a simpler one: will these cities will actually be good for the people who end up living in them? Abundance in the Desert Millions of units already built, sitting completely empty. Imagine snapping your fingers and emptying Manhattan. Source . We spent an hour trying to reach the residential districts. Every street eventually gave way to rubble about a kilometer short of the entrance, with highway signs pointing toward destinations that went nowhere, like this: What I could see from a distance wasn’t horrible. The buildings weren’t crammed together but weren’t too sprawling either (something close to what urban planners might call gentle density ), and there were hints of genuine architectural thought. items.summaryZh: 文章记录了作者在埃及新首都的实地考察，指出这座由中国等外资大规模建设的“新行政首都”充斥着未完工的建筑、废墟和低人口使用率，质疑其是否真正惠及当地居民以及背后的地缘经济风险。
items.translationZh:
标题：关于埃及的注记

正文：来源。当我的司机接我去新首都时，他确信出现了预订错误。“你确定要走这条路吗？我可以带你去金字塔或亚历山大。”并没有错误。但在路上，经过每隔几百米就能看到一次总统西西的壁画（戴着太阳镜，指着他新巨型城市的渲染图），我不知道该期待什么。仅凭网上阅读很难真正了解新首都。几乎所有图片都是经过空气刷或仅拍摄天际线，旁边的瓦砾并未显示。连名字都让人困惑。经过多年竞争、评审和如“Kemet”“Wedian”“Memphis”等拟名后，这座城市仍然技术上仅称为“新行政首都”。开罗并不像雅加达那样下沉，且没人能确定新首都的真实动机。官方理由是缓解约2000万人口的都会区拥堵。是的，开罗部分地区确实密集拥挤，但也有相当宽敞舒适的区域。新首都并非仅是开罗边缘的便利附属，它位于沙漠中，驱车前往花了一个小时，司机说“如果有交通的话，两小时”。在开斋节之夜，开罗充斥着渴望与迷茫外国人练习英语的少年。于是我问他们原因。根据我的正式街头调查，青少年的最常见回答是“西西想显示他有大男子主义”。有人说他想像建造吉萨金字塔的胡夫一样。一位在开罗一家内衣店工作的女士带着历史性的无奈说：“埃及人一直喜欢在荒郊野外砸钱建大工程”。一个相对常见的说法是，西西把首都建得远离中心、道路宽阔，以防止革命。密集的城市如开罗，群众可以包围政府大楼，但在拥有八车道超级高速公路的首都，要发起抗议几乎需要一辆车。还有人指出，这里是富人逃离的地方。这在新首都附近的封闭郊区Madinaty（马迪纳蒂）表现最为明显，驾车穿过时让人怀疑自己是否仍在埃及，还是已经进入了南加州的郊区。Madinaty，距离开罗45分钟的精英郊区。照片未显示其被沙漠包围。来源。“没关系，”一位穿尘土西装的男子说，“中国正在为我们建造”。进入新首都[《沙丘》主题曲响起]新首都的入口是一扇巨大的大门，从四周的沙丘中拔地而起。无论你在新首都走到哪儿，都可以用同一句话概括：最大的、最炫目的建筑，四周环绕着沙子和瓦砾。入口大门。来源。“那是非洲最大的清真寺，”我的司机指着从沙中耸立的红木般的两座宣礼塔说。我想停下来拍照，他既没有走下一个匝道，也没有靠路肩停车，只是把车在高速公路中间停下，我下车拍照。清真寺空无一人。非洲最大清真寺。来源。我们还经过：体育城——为埃及的奥运和世界杯申办提供支撑，拥有超过22个设施，包括约94,000座位的体育场，是埃及最大的、非洲第二大的体育场。体育城。来源。知识城——价值9.5亿美元的科技园区，政府称其为未来数字经济的节点（让人联想到北京的中关村）。知识城。来源。西西的新总统府——巨大的盘形建筑，据称采用古埃及太阳神的造型。总统府。来源。无尽排排的政府部门办公楼——每座建筑庞大且镀金，有的职能如此细分，以致难以想象有多少员工能填满一层，更别说这些巨楼。绿河——首都的公园，草坪在沙漠中发出绿色光芒，面积是中央公园的六倍。绿河概念图。来源。将这些纪念性建筑统一起来的主题是覆盖在它们上的风沙，使白色外墙显得像是油漆尚未干透。废墟和未完工的建筑向四面延伸，流浪狗在高速路中间睡觉。我的电影对比：《银翼杀手2049》。这在市中心也同样真实，那里聚集了一批摩天大楼，包括非洲最高的建筑，由中国建造。非洲最高建筑。中国

这座城市最初计划由阿联酋商人领衔的私营房地产公司Capital City Partners建造，但该合作在2015年破裂。随后埃及与中国建筑工程总公司（CSCEC）签署新协议，建造并融资行政核心，尽管CSCEC最终仅参与中央商务区。欧洲公司也参与其中。西门子负责大部分电力基础设施，单轨铁路由法国阿尔斯通（Alstom）建造，尽管中国仍是最大参与者。新首都被宣传为“智慧城市”，实际上意味着预先布设监控基础设施、数据整合，甚至未来可能的自动驾驶车辆。我之前写过，中国在国内外的自动驾驶车辆（AV）布局，不仅是输出汽车，更是输出完整的基础设施体系，重新塑造建筑环境以配合技术，而不是让技术适应已有环境。虽然我在新首都没有看到中国的自动驾驶车辆（实际上也没看到太多车辆），但道路本身已经具备基础设施。与开罗的喇叭声混乱相比，这里的道路宽阔、标线清晰，交叉口装有摄像头和传感器。市中心的欢迎标志斑驳。这样的模式正成为常态。印度尼西亚在婆罗洲建设努桑塔拉以取代雅加达，理由同样是缓解旧城拥堵，智能城市和监控设施大量中国参与，且呈现同样的在建鬼城氛围——新总统悄悄将预算削减一半后更是如此。塞内加尔在达喀尔外30公里建造Diamniadio，同样有大量中国融资。马来西亚的森林城（Forest City）由中国公司碧桂园在靠近新加坡的填海地块开发，如今因马来西亚政治反弹和资本管制导致需求枯竭而空置。（据称森林城并非针对本地马来西亚人，而是面向想在国外置业的上层中国买家，因其海滨物业相对上海等沿海城市更为可负担。）中国当然也在本土这么做。雄安新区——2017年习近平个人城市遗产项目，位于北京西南约100公里，旨在承接首都非必要功能，最终容纳500万人口。七年后进展并不理想。公平地说，也有成功的“新城”。哈萨克斯坦1997年将首都从阿拉木图迁至阿斯塔纳，如今基本可运作，这也可能是埃及政府寻求其规划经验的原因。但关键问题是：如果这些城市未能成功，谁来承担成本？对于埃及等国家，答案显而易见。对中国来说，情况更为复杂。国有企业建筑公司拿钱建造，却不负责让城市有人居住。它们的收入在项目初期已实现，城市是否真的填满居民，主要是东道国的责任。“一带一路”、数字丝绸之路等框架协议与谅解备忘录，最合理的解释不是宏大战略，而是在人类历史上最大规模的建筑机器在国内已经无事可建时的去向。对中国的风险主要在东道国无法偿付债务时（正如斯里兰卡在违约后将一座战略港口以99年租赁方式交给中国国有企业的案例），但即便如此，中国往往能够争取让步、重新谈判有利条款，或直接将损失视为保持建筑业就业和政治关系温度的代价。在埃及的案例中，这一点已初现端倪。2023年，中国将超过94亿美元的埃及债务转换为发展项目与投资，并在此过程中获得了一些国有资产。如果这真是中国的世纪，那么此类大规模建设就是其纹理。宏观地缘经济问题值得讨论，但在我驶过成千上万的空置住宅单元时，我回到一个更简单的问题：这些城市真的会对最终居住在那里的民众有益吗？

沙漠中的丰裕——数百万已建成的单元却完全空置。想象一下，只要打个响指就把曼哈顿清空。来源。我们花了一个小时试图进入住宅区。每条街道最终都在入口约一公里处变成瓦砾，路标指向的目的地根本不存在，如此情形。远观并不糟糕。建筑并未堆得紧凑，也没有过度蔓延（接近城市规划师所谓的温和密度），而且有真实的建筑思考痕迹。檐口、退台、细节……
